attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- artifacts-builder: Suite of tools for creating elaborate, multi-component claude.ai HTML artifacts using modern frontend web technologies (React, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui). Use for complex artifacts requiring state management, routing, or shadcn/ui components - not for simple single-file HTML/JSX artifacts.
- awesome-changelog: Use when generating a changelog or release notes — analyzes git history, categorizes commits, and produces customer-friendly release notes from technical commit messages.
- awesome-mcp-builder: Use when creating a new MCP server — four-phase process with deep research, agent-centric design, implementation, review, and evaluation creation. No shortcuts on API research.
- awesome-skills-catalog: Use when looking for a pre-built skill for a specific task — reference catalog of all skills from ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills covering documents, dev tools, business, communication, and 1000+ app integrations.
- awesome-webapp-testing: Use when testing local web applications — provides Playwright automation patterns, server lifecycle management, and best practices for end-to-end testing of Next.js apps.
- claude-code: Complete reference for Claude Code CLI — commands, flags, settings, hooks, MCP, skills, sub-agents, permissions, and all configuration options. Use when answering any question about Claude Code features, setup, or usage.
- superpowers-brainstorming: Use when starting any new feature, page, or component — forces design-before-code with structured spec writing, alternatives presentation, and user approval gate before any implementation begins.
- superpowers-code-review: Use when completing a task or feature — request code review via code-reviewer subagent with BASE_SHA and HEAD_SHA, then respond to feedback with technical reasoning not social compliance.
- superpowers-debugging: Use when debugging any issue — enforces root cause investigation before fixes. No trial-and-error patching. Systematic tracing, hypothesis testing, and failing test before fix.
- superpowers-execute-plan: Use when executing a written plan file — loads plan, raises concerns first, executes tasks with progress tracking, stops on blockers, runs verification after all tasks complete.
- superpowers-finish-branch: Use when development work on a feature branch is complete — verifies tests pass, then presents exactly four options: merge locally, push PR, keep as-is, or discard.
- superpowers-git-worktrees: Use when starting implementation work on a new feature — creates isolated git worktree, verifies baseline tests pass, sets up dependencies before any code changes.
- superpowers-parallel-agents: Use when facing 3+ independent failures or investigation tasks across separate domains — dispatch one agent per domain simultaneously instead of sequential investigation.
- superpowers-subagent-dev: Use when executing an implementation plan — dispatches specialized subagents per task with mandatory two-stage review (spec compliance then code quality) before moving to next task.
- superpowers-tdd: Use when writing any new function, feature, or bug fix — enforces test-first development with no exceptions. Write failing test, watch it fail, write minimal code, refactor.
- superpowers-verify: Use before claiming any task, fix, or feature is complete — run fresh verification commands and show actual output as evidence. No completion claims without proof.
- superpowers-write-plan: Use when creating an implementation plan for a multi-step feature — produces detailed plans with exact file paths, complete code, TDD steps, and git commits per task. No placeholders allowed.
- theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
The actual project is at `C:\Users\user\Desktop\Macro App\macro-generator-main\macro-generator-main\` — the zip was extracted with a nested folder.

You have two options:

**Option 1 — Run from the correct directory:**
```
cd "C:\Users\user\Desktop\Macro App\macro-generator-main\macro-generator-main"
npm run dev
```

**Option 2 — Move the files up one level** (cleaner, one-time fix):
```
cd "C:\Users\user\Desktop\Macro App\macro-generator-main"
move macro-generator-main\* .
move macro-generator-main\.* . 2>nul
rmdir macro-generator-main
```

Option 1 is the quickest fix right now. The nested folder happened because the zip archive already contained a folder named `macro-generator-main` and it was extracted into a folder of the same name.
